title.english: Kaitiakitanga of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream: Language as a Tool for Protection
title.teReoMāori: Te Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku: He Rauemi te Reo mō te Whakamarumaru

subject: Te Reo Māori
culturalContext: This lesson is deeply rooted in our local awa, Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, and the pūrākau of Hinepūkohurangi, the mist maiden associated with our valley. Students will explore how te reo Māori provides the conceptual framework for understanding and fulfilling our kaitiaki responsibilities to this specific waterway, connecting to Ngāti Kahungunu traditions of environmental guardianship.
learningObjectives: Identify and use at least 10 te reo Māori terms related to freshwater ecosystems (e.g., waipuna, kōawa, paruparu) in context
Compose a short karakia or waiata in te reo Māori specifically for Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Conduct and record a simple mihi in te reo Māori introducing themselves as kaitiaki of the Mangakōtukutuku
Create a bilingual (te reo Māori/English) informational sign about kaitiakitanga for our local awa

activities.title: He Mihimihi mō te Awa (Introducing Ourselves to the Stream)
activities.description: At the banks of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, students will learn and practice a mihi that introduces themselves, their maunga, and their awa, formally establishing their relationship as kaitiaki. They will record these using tablets.

activities.title: Te Kupu o te Taiao (The Language of the Environment)
activities.description: Using laminated vocabulary cards and guided by a local kaumātua, students will engage in a 'kupu hunt' (scavenger hunt) to match te reo Māori terms (e.g., waipuna - spring, kōawa - stream, kōura - crayfish) with their physical counterparts in and around the awa.

activities.title: Hei Waiata, Hei Karakia (Composing a Waiata or Karakia)
activities.description: In small groups, students will collaborate to compose a simple, original waiata or karakia in te reo Māori. The composition will express gratitude to the awa and a commitment to its protection, using the vocabulary learned.

activities.title: Te Tohu Kaitiaki (The Kaitiaki Sign)
activities.description: Students will design and create a small, durable informational sign in both te reo Māori and English. The sign will explain the concept of kaitiakitanga and include specific actions people can take to care for Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, to be placed near the stream.

resources: Kuia Ringahora Davis (Local Ngāti Kahungunu Kaumātua and native speaker)
The 'Te Awa' learning resource from TKI Te Reo Māori
Laminated vocabulary cards specific to freshwater ecosystems
Tablets with voice recording apps for capturing mihi and waiata

tasks: Presentation of the composed waiata/karakia to the class
Completion of the bilingual kaitiaki sign

nzcAlignment: Te Reo Māori: Communication - Mana Reo (Level 4: Use basic te reo Māori grammar and vocabulary to communicate about immediate and familiar environments)
Te Reo Māori: Language Knowledge - Mana Reo (Level 4: Understand that te reo Māori is a living language connected to place and identity)
Social Sciences: Continuity and Change (Level 4: Understand how the ways in which leadership of groups is acquired and exercised have consequences for communities and societies)
